{{Short description|Men's national association football team representing Ukraine}}

Андреа Мальдера – головний тренер збірної України|language=uk|website=sport.ua|author=Микола Титюк|date=2026-05-18|accessdate=2026-05-18}}</ref>
| Captain            = [[Mykola Matviyenko]] 
| Most caps          = [[Anatoliy Tymoshchuk]] (144){{efn|name=annulled}}
| Top scorer         = [[Andriy Shevchenko]] ([[List of international goals scored by Andriy Shevchenko|48]])
| Home Stadium       = due to the [[Russo-Ukrainian War]] - [[#Stadiums|Various]] in other countries (Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Germany, Spain) 
| FIFA Trigramme     = UKR
| FIFA Rank          = {{FIFA World Rankings|UKR}}

| FIFA min date      = September 1993

| socks1             = F9E733

| socks2             = 085cb9
| First game         = {{Fb|UKR|1992}} [[1992 Ukraine v Hungary football match|1–3]] {{fb-rt|HUN}}<br>([[Uzhhorod]], Ukraine; 29 April 1992)
| Largest win        = {{Fb|UKR}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|SMR}}<br>([[Lviv]], Ukraine; 6 September 2013)
| Largest loss       = {{Fb|FRA}} 7–1 {{fb-rt|UKR}}<br>([[Saint-Denis, Seine-Saint-Denis|Saint-Denis]], France; 7 October 2020)
| World cup apps     = 1
| World cup first    = 2006
| World cup best     = Quarter-finals ([[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006]])
| Regional name      = [[UEFA European Championship|European Championship]]
| Regional cup apps  = 4
| Regional cup first = [[UEFA Euro 2012|2012]]
| Regional cup best  = Quarter-finals ([[UEFA Euro 2020|2020]])
}}
The '''Ukraine national football team''' ({{langx|uk|Збірна України з футболу|Zbirna Ukrainy z futbolu}}) represents [[Ukraine]] in men's international [[Association football|football]], and is governed by the [[Ukrainian Association of Football]], the governing body for [[football in Ukraine]]. Ukraine's home ground is the [[Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex|Olimpiyskiy Stadium]] in [[Kyiv]]. The team has been a full member of [[UEFA]] and [[FIFA]] since 1992.

After [[Declaration of Independence of Ukraine|the restoration of the Ukrainian independence]], the team played their [[1992 Ukraine v Hungary football match|first match]] against [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]] on 29 April 1992. In their debut in a major world championship, the team reached the quarter-finals in the [[2006 FIFA World Cup]].<ref name=UEFA>{{cite web|url=http://www.uefa.com/memberassociations/association=ukr/profile/index.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100209095227/http://www.uefa.com/memberassociations/association=UKR/profile/index.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=9 February 2010|title=Member associations - Ukraine - Profile |website=[[UEFA]]}}</ref> Ukraine is one of only three post-Soviet states to have qualified for the FIFA World Cup finals, the others being [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]] and [[Russia national football team|Russia]].

As the host nation, Ukraine automatically qualified for [[UEFA Euro 2012]].<ref name=UEFA/> Four years later, Ukraine finished third in their qualifying group for [[UEFA Euro 2016|Euro 2016]] and advanced for the first time, via the play-off route and qualifiers, to reach a [[UEFA European Championship]] tournament. This marked the first time in Ukraine's six play-off appearances that it managed to win a tie, having lost previous play-off ties for the [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998 World Cup]], [[UEFA Euro 2000|Euro 2000]], the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]], the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]] and the [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014 World Cup]], and would lose again in the [[2022 FIFA World Cup|2022]] and [[2026 FIFA World Cup|2026 World Cup]] play-offs.

Ukraine's best performances in the UEFA European Championship and in the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] were in [[UEFA Euro 2020|2020]] and [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006]] respectively, reaching the quarter-finals for the first time in both cases.

==History==

===Ukrainian SSR (1924–1990)===
The national team was formed in the early 1990s and was recognized internationally soon thereafter. Ukraine, under the [[Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic]], previously had a national team in 1924–1935<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.ukrainian-football.com/|title=Buy Replica Football & Soccer Jerseys from China|website=Ukrainian Football}}</ref><ref>[http://www.ukrsoccerhistory.com/index.aspx?page=nt_before1992 Ukrainian Soccer History website] {{in lang|uk}}</ref> just like the [[Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic]]. The national team included the players Andriy Ponomarenko, [[Ivan Privalov (footballer)|Ivan Privalov]], [[Volodymyr Fomin]], H. Syrota, [[Mykola Fomin]], Anatoliy Lisnyi, Oleksandr Shatokha (goalkeeper), Dmytro Kyryllov, Dmytro Starusev, Serhiy Kopeiko, Petro Parovyshnykov (first team); Valentyn Prokofyev, Fedir Tyutchev, H. Yakubovskyi, Ivan Vladymyrskyi, Serafim Moskvin (goalkeeper), Kazymyr Piontkovskyi, Mykhailo Pashuta, Vasiliy Yepishin, Adam Bem, K. Us, Volodymyr Prasolov (second team).<ref>[https://gx.net.ua/history-harkov/5-iyunya-v-istorii-harkova-otkryt-samyj-bolshoj-v-strane-stadion.html 5 июня в истории Харькова: открыт самый большой в стране стадион]. gx.net.ua. 5 June 2021</ref><ref>[http://dinamo.kharkiv.ua/ru/istoriya-6/ САМЫЕ ПЕРВЫЕ ШАГИ]. dinamo.kharkiv.ua</ref>

The earliest record of games played by Ukraine can be traced back to August 1928. A championship among the national teams of the Soviet republics as well as the [[Moscow]] city team took place in Moscow; at the All-Soviet tournament, Ukraine reached the final where it lost to Moscow 1–0, after defeating [[Belarus national football team|Belarus]] and [[Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic|Transcaucasus]].

In 1929, Ukraine beat [[Lower Austria]] in an exhibition match in [[Kharkiv]] 4–1, and played in another Soviet tournament. Ukraine lost to Transcaucasus 3–0.

===Official formation===
Before 1991, Ukrainian players were represented by the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union national team]]. After the collapse of the [[Soviet Union|USSR]] in 1991, [[Russia national football team|Russia]] took its place in the qualifying tournament for the 1994 World Cup. The Ukraine national team did not enter the tournament<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://globalinfo4.com/|title=Home - Global Info4|date=2 March 2023|access-date=26 May 2023|archive-date=12 June 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240612190808/https://globalinfo4.com/|url-status=dead}}</ref> as it was not yet admitted to FIFA. Meanwhile, some of the best Ukrainian players of the 1990s (including [[Andrei Kanchelskis]], [[Viktor Onopko]], [[Sergei Yuran]], [[Yuriy Nikiforov]], [[Ilya Tsymbalar]] and [[Oleg Salenko]]) chose to play for Russia. At the time, [[Vyacheslav Koloskov]], a top official from the former Soviet Union and later [[Russia]], served as a vice-president of [[UEFA]] from 1980–1996 representing the Soviet Union and later the [[Commonwealth of Independent States]] (CIS).
[[File:Valeri Lobanovsky.jpg|200px|thumb|[[Valeriy Lobanovskyi]] was Head Coach of the National Team in 1979 and between 2001 and 2002.]]
The Soviet Union's five-year [[UEFA coefficient]] (earned in part by Ukrainian players), was transferred to the Russian national team. As a result, a crisis was created for both the national team and the [[Ukrainian Premier League|domestic league]].

Controversy developed because the Ukrainian football league lacked adequate funding for their teams<ref name=kmcrossing/> due to the economic crisis affecting all CIS countries.<ref name=kmcrossing/> There also was a reverse influx of players;<ref name=kmcrossing/> [[Viktor Leonenko]] agreed on transfer from [[Dynamo Moscow]] to [[FC Dynamo Kyiv|Dynamo Kyiv]]. The Russian club did not want to release him, but Leonenko did not want to continue to play in Moscow.<ref name=kmcrossing/>

In the following years, the Ukrainian team improved, including talents like [[Andriy Shevchenko]], [[Serhiy Rebrov]], and [[Oleksandr Shovkovskyi]].

===First official games (Prokopenko)===
In 1992, Ukraine was accepted as a full member to [[FIFA]] and [[UEFA]] at which time Ukraine selected its first manager by members of a coaching council which consisted of [[Anatoliy Puzach]] (manager of [[FC Dynamo Kyiv|Dynamo Kyiv]]), [[Yevhen Kucherevskyi]] ([[FC Dnipro]]), [[Yevhen Lemeshko]] ([[FC Torpedo Zaporizhzhia|Torpedo Zaporizhzhia]]), [[Yukhym Shkolnykov]] ([[FSC Bukovyna Chernivtsi|Bukovyna Chernivtsi]]) and [[Viktor Prokopenko]] ([[FC Chornomorets Odesa|Chornomorets Odesa]]). Later, they were joined by [[Valeriy Yaremchenko]] ([[FC Shakhtar Donetsk|Shakhtar Donetsk]]). The circle was narrowed to three specialists and Prokopenko eventually became the manager.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://en.uaf.ua/article/33444|title=The first match in the history of the national team of Ukraine: how it was 29 years ago ... - Official site of Ukrainian Football Association|website=en.uaf.ua|access-date=1 May 2021|archive-date=28 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220928103034/https://en.uaf.ua/article/33444|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Настя Федоренко. Донецк. Увидеть и полюбить. ФК Шахтер 051.jpg|thumb|left|[[Viktor Prokopenko]], the first official manager of the national team]]

Ukraine played their first match on 29 April 1992 against Hungary in [[Uzhhorod]] at the [[Avanhard Stadium (Uzhhorod)|Avanhard Stadium]], losing 3–1 with the sole Ukrainian goal scored by [[Ivan Hetsko]]. With the creation of a "phantom" (transitional) [[CIS national football team|CIS team]] in place of the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union]] playing against [[England national football team|England]] in Moscow in preparation for the [[UEFA Euro 1992]].<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150726235449/http://www.rusteam.permian.ru/history/1992.html 1992 season of the Russian national football tean]. Rusteam.permian.ru</ref> That year the Ukrainian team lost some notable players to the Soviet Union team. Following several losses to Hungary and a draw to the United States, Prokopenko resigned and the last season game was led by his assistants [[Mykola Pavlov]] and [[Leonid Tkachenko (footballer)|Leonid Tkachenko]].

===Euro 1996 qualification (Bazylevych)===
{{main|UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying Group 4}}
Ukraine appointed head coach, [[Oleh Bazylevych]], who made his debut with the national team in the spring of 1993 in [[Odesa]] during a friendly game against [[Israel national football team|Israel]], resulting in a 1–1 draw. Less than one month later, Ukraine finally won in [[Vilnius]] against Lithuania. During that summer they lost 3–1 to Croatia; Ukraine was later seeded in [[UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying#Group 4|Group 4]] of the [[UEFA Euro 1996]] qualification.

Ukraine was defeated by Israel in March 1994, and drew Bulgaria and the United Arab Emirates. On 7 September 1994, the national team started its first official qualification campaign with a home loss 2–0 to Lithuania.<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20161029181244/http://fanat.ua/national/15.html In captivity of emotions and ambitions (В плену у эмоций и амбиций)]}}. Fanat (from Komanda newspaper).</ref> Following the defeat and a weak performances in preceding friendlies, Bazylevych was fired and the tour to South Korea was led by the Bazylevych assistants [[Mykola Pavlov|Pavlov]] and [[Volodymyr Muntyan|Muntyan]]. Soon thereafter the Federation signed a contract with [[Valeriy Lobanovsky]]. On 24 September, the [[Ukrainian Association of Football|Football Federation of Ukraine]] appointed [[Yozhef Sabo]] as an acting manager until the end of the year after Lobanovsky signed a contract with [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]].

Under Sabo's management, the team's next home game against [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]] ended goalless<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20090330205030/http://fanat.ua/national/18.html Slovenians surprised and got surprised (Словенцы удивили и удивились)]}}. Komanda newspaper (by Fanat).</ref> and they eventually beat Estonia 3–0, gaining their first win in an official competitive game.<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20090330205445/http://fanat.ua/national/20.html Hopes are new, yet result is erstwhile (Надежды новые, результат прежний)]}}. Komanda newspaper (by Fanat)</ref> At the beginning of the year the Football Federation confirmed [[Anatoliy Konkov]] as the new head coach on 5 January 1995.
[[File:Oleg Blokhin-ua.jpeg|thumb|left|[[Oleg Blokhin]], two spells in charge of the Ukraine national team]]
Under coach Konkov, the team started with away losses of 4–0 to Croatia and 3–0 to Italy.<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20090401062838/http://fanat.ua/national/21.html To make &#91;necessary&#93; conclusions and &#91;continue&#93; to work (Сделать выводы и работать)]}}. Komanda newspaper (by Fanat)</ref> Following the losses, there was a three-game winning streak including a home victory against Croatia. A subsequent loss to Slovenia had the team finishing in fourth place in its first qualification campaign behind Lithuania.

===1998–2004: near misses===
Following Konkov's departure in 1996, the Federation appointed Sabo as head coach. Separately, there was a preliminary agreement with Lobanovsky to coach following his contract end with Kuwait.

Ukraine participated in [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|1998 World Cup qualification]], where the team was drawn into [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 9|Group 9]]. Ukraine took second place, behind [[Germany national football team|Germany]] and ahead of [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]], but was defeated in a play-off stage 3–1 on aggregate by [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]]. The qualification campaign became notable as the beginning of the international career of [[Andriy Shevchenko|Shevchenko]] as well as providing more play time for players such as [[Oleksandr Shovkovsky]] and [[Serhiy Rebrov]].

In the [[UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying]], Ukraine, assigned in [[UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying Group 4|Group 4]], finished above [[Russia national football team|Russia]], thanks to an important draw in [[Moscow]] and a home victory. However, they still only qualified for the playoff behind the [[France national football team|French side]] despite being undefeated. Ukraine then fell to [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]] 3–2 on aggregate. Following the qualification campaign, the Federation finally signed a contract with [[Valery Lobanovsky]], ending Sabo's tenure as a head coach.

The [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|2002 FIFA World Cup qualification]] saw Ukraine in [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 5|Group 5]]. With Lobanovsky as a head coach, there were expectations of the first qualification to the finals. Yet, Ukraine suffered a home loss to [[Poland national football team|Poland]] in their opening match, and many draws resulted in Ukraine qualifying for the playoff, losing to Germany, 5–2 on aggregate. Under public pressure, particularly the [[Ukrainian Premier League|Higher League]], head coaches argued for the removal of Lobanovsky and the Federation decided not to renew its contract with Lobanovsky, instead moving him to [[FC Dynamo Kyiv|Dynamo Kyiv]].

In the [[UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying]], Ukraine with the new head coach [[Leonid Buryak]], was assigned into [[UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying Group 6|Group 6]], with [[Spain national football team|Spain]] and [[Greece national football team|Greece]]. Ukraine failed to qualify.{{citation needed|date=April 2022}}

===2006 World Cup===
After the [[UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying|Euro 2004 qualifying]] match, Ukraine appointed [[Oleg Blokhin]] as the national team's head coach. Placed in [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 2|Group 2]], Ukraine went on to qualify as a group winner for their first-ever [[FIFA World Cup]] on 3 September 2005, after drawing 1–1 against [[Georgia national football team|Georgia]] in [[Tbilisi]] (and ahead of Turkey), playing Denmark and their last campaign against rivals Greece (among others). This was the first successful qualification campaign for Ukraine despite a poor home turf performance.

In the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]], Ukraine was in [[2006 FIFA World Cup Group H|Group H]] with [[Spain national football team|Spain]], [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] and [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]]. After losing 4–0 in the first match against Spain, the Ukrainians won the next two matches to face [[Switzerland national football team|Switzerland]] in round 16. Drawing goalless, Ukraine took Switzerland to a penalty shoot-out where two saves from [[Oleksandr Shovkovsky]] secured a positive outcome for his side despite the first kick miss by [[Andriy Shevchenko]]. Switzerland which did not lose or yield a single goal was sent home early with Ukraine advancing to the quarterfinals. In the quarterfinals, Ukraine, facing [[Italy national football team|Italy]], was defeated with two second half two goals from [[Luca Toni]], securing a comfortable 3–0 win for the future 2006 World Cup champions.

===2006–2012===
After the World Cup, Ukraine was placed in [[UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group B]], along with [[Italy national football team|Italy]] and [[France national football team|France]]; Ukraine had also performed poorly against [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]], [[Georgia national football team|Georgia]] and [[Lithuania national football team|Lithuania]], ultimately finishing in fourth place. Due to the bleak performance of the national team [[Oleg Blokhin]] resigned and surprisingly signed with the recently established [[FC Moscow]].<ref>[https://archive.today/20120729023939/http://uk.reuters.com/article/worldFootballNews/idUKL0689781720071206 Soccer-Blokhin quits as Ukraine coach] by Mikhail Volobuyev, Gennady Fyodorov and Ken Ferris, [[Reuters]], 6 December 2007</ref><ref>[https://archive.today/20120717161206/http://soccernet.espn.go.com/news/story?id=490600&cc=5739 FK Moscow hire former Ukraine manager Blokhin] ESPNsoccernet 14 December 2007</ref>

With another Soviet football star player [[Oleksiy Mykhaylychenko]] as the new head coach, [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|2010 FIFA World Cup qualification]] saw Ukraine in [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 6|Group 6]], drawing [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]] and winning against [[England national football team|England]], sending Ukraine to the playoff. Greece, which had been eliminated by Ukraine in the qualifiers four years earlier, would eventually get revenge.{{citation needed|date=April 2022}} Following the failure to qualify, the Federation decided not to renew the contract with Mykhaylychenko.<ref name=formerUK>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyivpost.com/news/nation/detail/55788/|title=Ukraine decides not to renew coach's contract|work=Kyiv Post|date=23 December 2009|accessdate=16 February 2021}}</ref>

{{Main|UEFA Euro 2012 Group D}}

[[File:Ukraine national football team 20120611.jpg|thumb|right|240px|Ukraine in 2012]]
[[File:Ukraine national team in 2012.jpg|240px|thumb|right|Ukraine before a match against Bulgaria, 14 December 2012]]
As co-hosts, Ukraine qualified automatically for [[UEFA Euro 2012|Euro 2012]],<ref name=UEFA/> marking their debut in the [[UEFA European Championship]]. The Federation decided to appoint [[Myron Markevych]] to prepare and lead the national team in the Euro finals. However, following a few friendlies Markevych resigned due to the off-pitch politics and having held coaching office of both the national team and [[FC Metalist Kharkiv|Metalist Kharkiv]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://nl.uefa.com/uefaeuro2012/news/newsid=1518482.html|title=Markevich leaves Ukraine helm on UEFA.COM|date=4 September 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100904235010/http://nl.uefa.com/uefaeuro2012/news/newsid=1518482.html |archive-date=4 September 2010 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyivpost.com/post/10118|title=Akhmetov claims Surkis not doing his job professionally|website=Get the Latest Ukraine News Today - KyivPost|date=18 February 2010 }}</ref> For the next several games in 2010 and 2011 the national team was led by caretaker [[Yuriy Kalitvintsev]] who starred for Ukraine back in its first qualification campaign for the Euro 1996.

On 21 April 2011, Blokhin was again appointed head coach of the Ukraine national team signing a four-year contract.<ref name="Blokhin2011A"/><ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20110426133526/http://football.uk.reuters.com/leagues/european/news/2011/04/21/LDE73K1AD.php Oleg Blokhin appointed Ukraine coach], [[Reuters]] (21 April 2011)</ref> With Blokhin at helm in their opening game against [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]], Ukraine won 2–1 in Kyiv. In Donetsk, Ukraine was eliminated after a 2–0 loss to France and a 1–0 defeat to England.

===2014–present===
Drawn into [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group H|UEFA Group H]] for [[2014 World Cup]] qualification, Ukraine played against France, beating France at home 2–0 but suffered a 3–0 loss away. Blokhin stepped down due to health concerns in the autumn of 2012 soon after the first home game against [[England national football team|England]] and was replaced by [[Andriy Bal]]<ref>[https://www.ua-football.com/ukrainian/national/1349536811-andriy-bal-priznacheniy-v-o-golovnogo-trenera-zbirnoyi-ukrayini.html Andriy Bal is appointed an acting head coach of Ukraine national team (Андрій Баль призначений в.о. головного тренера збірної України)]. UA-Football. 6 October 2012</ref> and later [[Oleksandr Zavarov]]. 

In 2012, the Federation appointed [[Mykhailo Fomenko]] as head coach.<ref>[https://www.kyivpost.com/article/content/sport/ukraines-football-federation-taps-fomenko-to-coach-national-team-318175.html Ukraine’s football federation taps Fomenko to coach national team].</ref> Even though the team under Fomenko did not manage to qualify for the World Cup, the Federation decided to retain his services until the end of 2015.<ref>[https://en.interfax.com.ua/news/sport/183175.html Fomenko to continue working with Ukraine's national football team until end of 2015].</ref>

[[File:Ukraine vs Luxembourg 14062015 UEFA EURO 2016 Qualifying round - Group C (6).jpg|thumb|right|240px|Ukraine in 2015]]
During ongoing [[Russo-Ukrainian War|conflicts with Russia]], Ukraine in [[UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying|Euro 2016 qualifying]] was drawn against Spain, [[Slovakia national football team|Slovakia]], Belarus, [[North Macedonia national football team|Macedonia]] and [[Luxembourg national football team|Luxembourg]]. Despite having won all matches except those against Spain and Slovakia, Ukraine finished third. They defeated [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]] in the playoff, marking their first qualification for a major tournament through the playoffs.

Ukraine lost all three games at Euro 2016 without scoring a goal.

Following the Euro 2016, Fomenko was replaced with Andriy Shevchenko as head coach (who was his assistant during the Euro finals).<ref>[https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/36803667 Andriy Shevchenko: Ukraine appoint former AC Mlian and Chelsea striker as manager].</ref> Seeded in the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group I|UEFA Group I]], Ukraine started with a home draw to [[Iceland national football team|Iceland]] in 2018 World Cup qualifying and an away draw to [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]] but lost to Croatia failing to qualify for the playoffs for the first time since [[UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying|UEFA Euro 2004]].

In the [[2018–19 UEFA Nations League B|inaugural UEFA Nations League]], Ukraine was drawn with the [[Czech Republic national football team|Czech Republic]] and Slovakia in League B. They beat the Czech Republic 2–1 away and Slovakia 1–0 at home, before earning a promotion to League A with a 1–0 home win to the Czech Republic.

==== UEFA Euro 2020 ====
{{main|UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying Group B}}
Ukraine was placed in a group with Euro 2016 champions [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]] as well as [[Serbia national football team|Serbia]]. 

As the [[COVID-19]] crisis in [[COVID-19 pandemic in Ukraine|Ukraine]] worsened, eight players from the starting squad tested positive (including one positive [[Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2|SARS-CoV-2]] test upon arrival to [[Lucerne]]), as a result the entire delegation was put into quarantine by the Department of Health of the [[Canton of Lucerne]].<ref>{{cite news |last=Hlyvynskyi |first=Oleksandr |url=https://en.uaf.ua/article/40745 |title=Official: League of Nations game Switzerland - Ukraine canceled - Official site of the Ukrainian Football Association |work=[[Ukrainian Association of Football]] |date=17 November 2020 |access-date=17 November 2020 |archive-date=11 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201211195213/https://en.uaf.ua/article/40745 |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-17 |title=Ukraine's UEFA Nations League fixture with Switzerland cannot take place |url=https://www.uefa.com/news-media/news/0263-10e05d13e774-cce7ae659596-1000--ukraine-s-uefa-nations-league-fixture-with-switzerland-ca/ |access-date=2024-06-19 |website=[[UEFA]] |language=en}}</ref> Their game against Switzerland away was subsequently cancelled. UEFA decided that the match result would be 3–0 in favour of Switzerland and the [[Court of Arbitration for Sport|CAS]] subsequently upheld the result, meaning that Ukraine had been officially relegated after just one year in League A.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-25 |title=UEFA ruling relegates Ukraine from Nations League top tier |url=https://apnews.com/uefa-ruling-relegates-ukraine-from-nations-league-top-tier-cb18f0bf30c00261f4508be13e42f2ac |access-date=2024-06-19 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-03-19 |title=Ukraine legal loss to UEFA means Nations League relegation |url=https://apnews.com/article/europe-ukraine-international-soccer-coronavirus-pandemic-switzerland-85ce35af457a24b25a1ce91d42ac9638 |access-date=2024-06-19 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref>

{{main|UEFA Euro 2020}}
Ukraine qualified for the knockout stages in the European Championship as one of the best third-placed teams for the first time in the 2020 tournament, postponed to 2021 due to [[COVID-19 pandemic in Europe|COVID-19]].  They beat Sweden 2–1 in the round of 16,and were then defeated by England in the quarter-final, recording their best finish at a major tournament since 2006.

In August 2021, Shevchenko<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 August 2021 |title=Shevchenko announces end of Ukraine contract |url=https://www.marca.com/en/football/international-football/2021/08/01/6106ca0c46163f59018b4589.html |website=MARCA}}</ref> was replaced with [[Oleksandr Petrakov]]. In the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group D|2022 World Cup qualifying]] rounds, Ukraine drew 1–1 in both games against France.<ref>{{Cite web|title=France-Ukraine: European Qualifiers|url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/match/2030762--france-vs-ukraine/|access-date=2021-03-25|website=[[UEFA]]|language=en}}</ref> Ukraine would qualify for the playoff after breaking the record set by [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] for the most consecutive draws in World Cup qualification, with five straight draws.{{citation needed|date=April 2022}} Ukraine eventually picked up a much-needed victory over Finland, ending their run of draws and giving them a two-point lead over Bosnia and a three-point lead over Finland. However, both Bosnia and Finland had a game in hand over Ukraine, who qualified for the playoffs after a 2–0 win over Bosnia and a Finnish loss to France. Ukraine faced [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]] in the Group A playoff semifinals, postponed in March 2022 to June after [[2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine|Russia invaded the country in February]],<ref name="Decisions on WC qualifiers">{{cite news |url=https://www.fifa.com/tournaments/mens/worldcup/qatar2022/media-releases/decisions-taken-concerning-fifa-world-cup-qatar-2022-tm-qualifiers |title=Decisions taken concerning FIFA World Cup Qatar 2022™ qualifiers |work=[[FIFA]] |date=8 March 2022 |access-date=8 March 2022}}</ref> winning 3–1 at [[Hampden Park]], but ultimately losing 1–0 to [[Wales national football team|Wales]] in an emotional playoff final at the [[Cardiff City Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web |author=Dafydd Pritchard |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/60869800 |title=Wales 1–0 Ukraine: Wales overcame another monumental effort from Ukraine to qualify for their first World Cup since 1958 on a night of high emotion and drama in Cardiff |website=BBC Sport |date=5 June 2022 }}</ref>

Ukraine, who were [[2020–21 UEFA Nations League A#Group 4|relegated to League B]] in the [[2020–21 UEFA Nations League|2nd Nations League]] due to their last-place finish (2 wins to 4 losses), failed to gain promotion to League A in the following Nations League, finishing 2nd in their group in the [[2022–23 UEFA Nations League B|3rd Nations League]], beaten by Scotland against whom they lost in [[Glasgow]] in contrast to the play-off semi-final (0–3) and unable to beat the ''Tartan Army'' on neutral ground in the return leg (0–0).

==== UEFA Euro 2024 and 2026 FIFA World Cup qualifiers ====
In the [[UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group C|qualifiers for the Euro 2024]], Ukraine finished third in their highly competitive group, with four wins, two draws and two defeats. However, Italy finished ahead of the ''Zbirna'' only thanks to a favourable head-to-head record despite having the same number of points, while in the decisive Ukraine/Italy match (0–0) on neutral ground in [[Leverkusen]], Germany, a refereeing error was made to the Ukrainians' disadvantage with a penalty not whistled for an Italian foul at the end of match.<ref>{{cite web |date=21 November 2023 |title=Ukraine – Italie. Le penalty non sifflé contre la Nazionale déclenche la polémique en Italie |url=https://www.ouest-france.fr/euro/ukraine-italie-le-penalty-non-siffle-contre-la-nazionale-declenche-la-polemique-en-italie-7850ef1c-8876-11ee-a92a-0b4bc05f91ae |website=[[Ouest-France]]}}<!-- auto-translated from French by Module:CS1 translator --></ref>

Eligible for the play-offs as the best non-group winner, Ukraine initially defeated [[Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team|Bosnia]] 2–1 away in the [[UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs#Semi-finals_2|semi-finals of the Path B play-offs]] on March 21, 2024.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ukraine 2-1 Bosnia and Herzegovina (Mar 21, 2024) Game Analysis |url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/report/_/gameId/690329 |access-date=2024-05-14 |website=ESPN |language=en}}</ref> Five days later, on March 26, 2024, Ukraine qualified for [[Euro 2024]] in Germany, beating Iceland 2–1 in the [[UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs#Final_2|play-off final]] at the [[Wrocław Stadium]] in [[Poland]].<ref>{{cite web |date=26 March 2024 |title=L'Ukraine sera à l'Euro grâce à sa victoire sur l'Islande en barrages |url=https://www.lequipe.fr/Football/Actualites/L-ukraine-sera-a-l-euro-grace-a-sa-victoire-sur-l-islande/1457047 |website=[[L'Équipe]]}}<!-- auto-translated from French by Module:CS1 translator --></ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Ukraine joy as Mudryk sends them to Euro 2024 |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/68603223 |access-date=2024-05-14 |work=BBC Sport |language=en-GB}}</ref> In this decisive final, as in the semi-final against Bosnia, Ukraine conceded the opening goal on each occasion and managed to turn the match around, winning by a narrow margin at the very end. Having been drawn into Group E with Romania, Belgium and Slovakia, Ukraine finished level with all the other teams in the group on four points, but were knocked out on goal difference.

In the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification|2026 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], Ukraine was drawn in [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group D|Group D]] alongside [[France national football team|France]], [[Iceland]], and [[Azerbaijan national football team|Azerbaijan]]. The campaign began with a 2–0 defeat to France and a 1–1 draw with Azerbaijan, before a 5–3 away victory over Iceland, with goals scored by [[Ruslan Malinovskyi]], [[Oleksiy Hutsulyak]], [[Ivan Kalyuzhnyi]], and [[Oleh Ocheretko]]. A 2–1 home win against Azerbaijan was followed by a 4–0 defeat in the reverse fixture against France in [[Paris]]. Ukraine finished second place in the group with 10 points after a 2–0 victory over Iceland in Warsaw, thereby qualifying [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round|for the play-offs]].

On 26 March 2026, Ukraine faced [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]] in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round#Path B|play-off semi-final]] in [[Valencia]], Spain. Despite a late goal from [[Matviy Ponomarenko|Matvii Ponomarenko]] in the 90th minute, a [[hat-trick]] by [[Viktor Gyökeres]] resulted in a 3–1 defeat, ending Ukraine’s hopes of qualifying for the 2026 World Cup. On 22 April, the [[Ukrainian Association of Football]] announced that head coach [[Serhiy Rebrov]] had stepped down by mutual agreement.

On 18 May 2026, the president of the Ukrainian Association of Football, Andriy Shevchenko, announced the appointment of Italian manager [[Andrea Maldera]] as the new head coach. This made Maldera the first foreign coach in the history of the men's national team.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 May 2026 |title=Andrea Maldara is the head coach of the Ukraine national team |url=https://uaf.ua/en/news/andrea-maldara-is-the-head-coach-of-the-ukraine-national-team |access-date=18 May 2026 |website=uaf.ua |publisher=[[Ukrainian Association of Football]]}}</ref> One of his primary objectives as coach is to qualify for the [[UEFA Euro 2028]] and top Ukraine's group in the [[2026–27 UEFA Nations League#League B|upcoming Nations League]] scheduled for September 2026.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=18 May 2026 |title=L'Ucraina sceglie Andrea Maldera: è il primo ct straniero nella storia del Paese |url=https://www.sportmediaset.mediaset.it/calcio/l-ucraina-sceglie-andrea-maldera-e-il-primo-ct-straniero-nella-storia-del-paese_112241324-202602k.shtml |access-date=18 May 2026 |website=www.sportmediaset.mediaset.it |language=it}}</ref>

==Stadiums==
{{more|List of football stadiums in Ukraine}}
Most matches are held at Kyiv's [[Olimpiyskyi National Sports Complex]].

During the Soviet era (before 1991), only three stadiums in Ukraine were used for official games: 
*[[Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex|Olimpiysky NSC]] in Kyiv (known then as Republican Stadium) 
*[[Black Sea Shipping Company|BSS]] Central Stadium in Odesa, the predecessor of [[Chornomorets Stadium|Chornomorets]]
*[[Lokomotiv Republican Sports Complex|Lokomotiv Stadium]] in [[Simferopol]]

Since May 2022, due to the [[Russian invasion of Ukraine]], home game matches have been taking place in [[Łódź]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-05-13 |title=UKRAINE WILL PLAY TWO HOME MATCHES OF THE NATIONS LEAGUE IN LODZ IN JUNE |url=https://en.uaf.ua/article/45106 |access-date=28 October 2022 |archive-date=29 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129012821/https://en.uaf.ua/article/45106 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

===Home venue record===

<small>Since [[Ukraine v Hungary (29 April 1992)|Ukraine's first fixture]] (29 April 1992 vs. [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]]) they have played their home games at 11 different [[stadium]]s. </small>

:<small>Last updated: 11 November 2021. Statistics include official FIFA-recognised matches only.</small>

==Kits and sponsors==
===Kit history and evolution===
{{Commons|Ukraine national football team kits}} 
Before 5 February 2009, Ukraine wore a [[Lotto Sport Italia|Lotto]] kit, which was famously featured during their quarter-final run at the 2006 FIFA World Cup. The team subsequently transitioned to [[Adidas]], debuting a 2009 design characterized by a yellow base and a distinctive "snake sash" across the chest.<ref name="kits">{{cite web |url=https://www.footballshirtculture.com/09/10-kits/ukraine-2009-10-adidas-football-kits.html|title=Ukraine 09/10 Adidas football kits |date=6 February 2009 |access-date=11 June 2009 |work=footballshirtculture}}</ref> This design was replaced on 29 March 2010 by a more traditional adidas template, which served the team through Euro 2012 and Euro 2016.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.ua-football.com/ukrainian/national/4bb059d4.html |title=Новую форму сборной первым примерил Ракицкий (+фото) (New uniform for the National team was first fitted by Rakytsky with photo) |date=29 March 2010|work=ua.football|publisher=Globalinfo (Kyiv, Ukraine)|language=ru}}</ref> In 2017, the Ukrainian Association of Football (UAF) switched technical partners to the Spanish brand [[Joma]], a partnership that spanned seven years and included the team’s quarter-final appearance at Euro 2020. The Joma era concluded in late 2024, when Ukraine officially returned to Adidas as its exclusive supplier. The new partnership was inaugurated in October 2024 with the launch of the home and away kits for the 2024/25 season, which were first worn during the October window of the UEFA Nations League.<ref>{{Cite web |title=adidas unveils new home and away kits for Ukraine teams - Official website of the Ukrainian Association of Football |url=https://uaf.ua/en/article/52500 |access-date=2025-09-06 |website=Ukrainian Association of Football |language=en}}</ref>

[[File:Logo of Football Federation of Ukraine.svg|100px|thumb|right|Former crest]]

===Sponsors===
Marketing for the Football Federation of Ukraine is conducted by the Ukraine Football International (UFI).
* Title [[Sponsor (commercial)|sponsor]]: [[Epicentr K|Epicentr]] (since 2013)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://fakty.ictv.ua/ua/index/read-news/id/1467782|title=Спонсор збірної України пообіцяв $2 млн. за вихід на ЧС-2014 - Факти|date=22 January 2013}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://forbes.ua/profile/epitsentr-k-227|title=about the company epicenter|website=forbes.ua|date=November 2022 }}</ref><ref>{{YouTube|nz0g9vrmkYk|Presentation of new sponsors in 2013}}. Youtube channel of FFU.</ref>
* Premium (General) sponsors: Chernihivske (since 1998)
* Official sponsors: [[Henkel]] (Ukraine), [[Adidas]], Airline "MAU" ([[Ukraine International Airlines]]), NIKO (official [[Mitsubishi]] distributor in Ukraine), Boris clinic, Tour agency "Love Cyprus", Resort center "Grand Admiral Club"

Former title and general sponsors included [[Ukrtelecom]], [[Kyivstar]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ua-football.com/ukrainian/national/44855454.html|title=Спонсори збірної України, їх статуси і класифікація|first=Внешние|last=источники|date=6 June 2006}}</ref> Nordex (Austria),<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20211116182149/http://fanat.ua/programa/4_sborna/4_007.html Announcement of the game Ukraine vs Estonia]}}. Fanat.ua</ref><ref>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20090326041825/http://fanat.ua/national/12.html Ukraine 3:1 Belarus (Украина Белоруссия 3:1)]}}. Fanat.ua</ref> and Geoton.

{| class="wikitable" 
! Kit supplier
! Period 
|-
|{{flagicon|GBR}} [[Umbro]]
|1992–1997
|-

|1998–2002
|-

|2003–2008
|-
|{{flagicon|GER}} [[Adidas]] 
|2009–2016
|-
|{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Joma]] 
|2017–2024

|2024–present
|}

|attendance   = 
|referee      = [[Sigurd Kringstad]] ([[Norwegian Football Federation|Norway]])
|report       = https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/friendlies/match/2048234--denmark-vs-ukraine/
|result       = P
|note         = The match was abandoned after the 65th minute, following Denmark midfielder [[Christian Eriksen]] suffering an on-field collapse.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.skysports.com/football/news/11095/13551756/christian-eriksen-denmark-friendly-with-ukraine-abandoned-after-midfielder-collapses-on-pitch |title=Christian Eriksen: Denmark friendly with Ukraine abandoned after midfielder collapses on pitch |publisher=Sky Sports |date=7 June 2026 |access-date=7 June 2026}}</ref>
}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#Association football|friendly]] matches against [[Poland national football team|Poland]] and [[Denmark national football team|Denmark]] on 31 May and 7 June 2026, respectively.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://en.uaf.ua/news/cklad-zbirnoyi-ukrayiny-na-tovaryski-matchi-proty-komand-polshchi-i-daniyi| title=Ukraine national team lineup for friendly matches against Poland and Denmark|publisher=[[Ukrainian Association of Football]]|date=18 May 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of 7 June 2026, after the match against [[Denmark national football team|Denmark]].''<ref name="auto2">{{cite web|url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/196/2023/Ukraine.html|title=Ukraine (2021)|first=Benjamin|last=Strack-Zimmermann|website=National Football Teams}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/oekr-recintlp.html|title=Ukraine - Record International Players|website=RSSSF}}</ref><ref name="auto1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kopanyi-myach.info/index.aspx?page=zbirna_matches&mode=players&order=abc|title=Kopanyi-Myach.info - Літопис українського футболу|website=www.kopanyi-myach.info|access-date=8 June 2021|archive-date=8 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230608041431/http://kopanyi-myach.info/index.aspx?page=zbirna_matches&mode=players&order=abc|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto">{{Cite web|url=https://eu-football.info/_players.php?id=265&data=9|title=Most Ukraine Caps|website=EU-Football.info}}</ref>

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up for the team within the last 12 months.

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Yehor Yarmolyuk]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|3|1|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=0|club=[[Brentford F.C.|Brentford]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|POL}}, 31 May 2026 <sup>INJ</sup>}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Ivan Kalyuzhnyi]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|1998|1|21}}|caps=14|goals=1|club=[[FC Metalist 1925 Kharkiv|Metalist 1925 Kharkiv]]|clubnat=UKR|latest=v. {{fb|ALB}}, 31 March 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Oleksandr Pikhalyonok]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|1997|5|7}}|caps=13|goals=0|club=[[FC Dynamo Kyiv|Dynamo Kyiv]]|clubnat=UKR|latest=v. {{fb|ALB}}, 31 March 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Vladyslav Veleten]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|2002|10|1}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[FC Polissya Zhytomyr|Polissya Zhytomyr]]|clubnat=UKR|latest=v. {{fb|FRA}}, 13 November 2025 <sup>RES</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Oleksandr Zinchenko (footballer)|Oleksandr Zinchenko]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|1996|12|15}}|caps=75|goals=12|club=[[Free agent|''Unattached'']]|clubnat=|latest=v. {{fb|AZE}}, 9 September 2025 <sup>INJ</sup>}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Ihor Krasnopir]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|2002|12|1}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[FC Polissya Zhytomyr|Polissya Zhytomyr]]|clubnat=UKR|latest=v. {{fb|POL}}, 31 May 2026 <sup>RES</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=19|pos=FW|name=[[Vladyslav Vanat]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|2002|1|4}}|caps=17|goals=2|club=[[Girona FC|Girona]]|clubnat=ESP|latest=v. {{fb|SWE}}, 26 March 2026}}
{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>U21</sup> = Called up from national [[Ukraine national under-21 football team|U21]] squad.
*<sup>WD</sup> =  Withdrew from squad for reason other than injury.
*<sup>INJ</sup> = Not part of the [[#Current squad|current squad]] due to injury.
*<sup>RES</sup> = Reserve squad (replaces a member of the squad in case of injury/unavailability).
*<sup>RET</sup> = Retired from the national team.
*<sup>PRE</sup> = Preliminary squad/standby.
<!--*<sup>COVID</sup> = Not part of squad due to resting positive for [[Coronavirus disease 2019|COVID-19]].
*<sup>SUS</sup> = Not available for next match due to red card suspension.--->
{{nat fs end}}

:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Ukraine.''

==Competitive record==

===FIFA World Cup===
{{main|Ukraine at the FIFA World Cup}}
{{for|Ukrainians on the Soviet Union national football team|Ukrainians on the Soviet Union national football team}}
{{for|results before 1990|Soviet Union national football team}}
{{Color box|gold|border=darkgray}} '''Champions'''&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|silver|border=darkgray}} Runners-up&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|#cc9966|border=darkgray}} Third place&nbsp;&nbsp;

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
! colspan="10" |[[FIFA World Cup]] record
!style="width:1%;" rowspan="17"|
!colspan=8|[[FIFA World Cup qualification|Qualification]] record
|-
!Year
!Round
!Position
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}*
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}
!{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}
!Squad
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}
!{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}
!{{Tooltip|→|Article}}
!Outcome
|-
| [[1930 FIFA World Cup|1930]] to [[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990]]
| colspan="9" | ''Part of {{flagcountry|Soviet Union}}''
| colspan=8| ''Part of {{flagcountry|Soviet Union}}''
|-
! colspan="10" | as {{flagcountry|Ukraine}}
!colspan=8| as {{flagcountry|Ukraine}}
|-
|{{flagicon|United States}} [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994]]
| colspan="9" |''Not eligible to participate''.{{efn|name=omission|Ukraine was part of the [[Soviet Union]] until 1991. Following its independence, the country established its own football association and became a member of FIFA in 1992. FIFA adopted a decision not to allow to participate in the 1994 FIFA World Cup the national teams of those former Soviet republic that did not participate in the qualification draw on 8 December 1991.<ref name=kmcrossing>[http://www.kopanyi-myach.info/index.aspx?page=history11 At the crossing (На переправе)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200814063404/http://www.kopanyi-myach.info/index.aspx?page=history11 |date=2020-08-14 }}. Kopanyi myach.</ref> A proposition of Ukraine to arrange a separate tournament for all successors of the Soviet Union and supported by Georgia and Armenia was blocked by Russia.<ref name=hwta>{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20160305103050/http://fanat.ua/national/02.html We hacked window to America (Прорубили окно в Америку)]}}. Komanda newspaper (by Fanat)</ref>}}
|colspan=6|''Not eligible to participate.''{{efn|name=omission}}
|[[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|1994]] || align=left|Qualifying spot not granted by [[FIFA]]  [[File:Symbol delete vote.svg|17px]]
|-
|rowspan=2|{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998]]
| colspan="9" rowspan="3" |''Did not qualify''
|-
|12
|6
|3
|3
|11
|9
|[[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)#Group 9|1998]] || align=left|2nd in qualifying group 9, lost to [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]] 0:2 1:1(H) (agg.1-3) in [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round|play-off]]  [[File:Symbol delete vote.svg|17px]]
|-
|{{flagicon|South Korea|1997}} {{flagicon|Japan}} [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002]]

!–
!87
!41
!29
!17
!133
!76
! ||
|}

:''* Denotes draws include knock-out matches decided on penalty kicks.''

===UEFA European Championship===
{{main|Ukraine at the UEFA European Championship}}
{{for|results before 1988|Soviet Union national football team}} 
{{for|results 1992|CIS national football team}}
{{Color box|gold|border=darkgray}} '''Champions'''&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|silver|border=darkgray}} Runners-up&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|#cc9966|border=darkgray}} Third place&nbsp;&nbsp;

==Head-to-head record==
[[File:Ukraine national football team (matches with opponents).png|thumb|350px|World Map of Ukraine's opponents]]
The following table shows Ukraine's all-time international record, correct as of 7 June 2026.<ref>{{cite web |title=All matches |url=http://www.ffu.org.ua/ukr/teams/teams_main/a_history/ |work=ffu.org.ua |access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=All-time Ukraine national football team international record|url=https://eu-football.info/_matches.php?id=265|work=eu-football.info|access-date=23 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te1985/ukraine/|title=Ukraine - Historical results|website=worldfootball.net|date=25 March 2023 }}</ref>

{| class="wikitable" style="font-size:95%; text-align:center;"
|+'''Key'''
| style="background: #CCFFCC;" width="20" |
| bgcolor="#ffffff" align="left" | '''Positive balance''' (more wins)
|-
| style="background: #FFFFBB;" width="20" |
| bgcolor="#ffffff" align="left" | '''Neutral balance''' (equal W/L ratio)
|-
| style="background: #FFBBBB;" width="20" |
| bgcolor="#ffffff" align="left" | '''Negative balance''' (more losses)
|}

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="font-size:100%; text-align:center;"
